blocks.text: Hear this word that I take up over you in lamentation, O house of Israel:
Fallen, no more to rise, is maiden Israel; forsaken on her land, with no one to raise her up.
For thus says the Lord God: The city that marched out a thousand shall have a hundred left, and that which marched out a hundred shall have ten left.
5.1–3: Lament for Israel. Among the forms of prophetic speech was the lament; hearing one's own funeral speech delivered by a prophet must have had a sobering effect (cf. Jer 9.17–22).
For thus says the Lord to the house of Israel: Seek me and live;
but do not seek Bethel, and do not enter into Gilgal or cross over to Beer-sheba; for Gilgal shall surely go into exile, and Bethel shall come to nothing.
5 : For and , see 3.14n.; 4.4n. , though in the Negeb to the far south, was a shrine associated with Israel's ancestors (Gen 21.25–31; 26.23; 46.1–4) and would have attracted pilgrims from the north.
Seek the Lord and live, or he will break out against the house of Joseph like fire, and it will devour Bethel, with no one to quench it.
6 : , (i.e., the Northern Kingdom). Its two main parts were Ephraim and Manasseh, named for the sons of Joseph (Gen 41.50–52; 48).
Ah, you that turn justice to wormwood, and bring righteousness to the ground!

5.4–7: A plea for repentance. Though the lament form was meant to convey a sense of inevitable judgment, there is yet time to (v. 6), an exhortation to turn to God or abide by God's precepts (Ps 24.6; Prov 28.5; Isa 55.6). The prophet enjoins Israel to the , not the sanctuaries of the .
7 : The dried leaves of were aromatic and bitter; here, injustice leads to bitterness (see 6.12).

The one who made the Pleiades and Orion, and turns deep darkness into the morning, and darkens the day into night, who calls for the waters of the sea, and pours them out on the surface of the earth, the Lord is his name,
8 : and also occur together in Job 9.9; 38.31. Though astral bodies could inspire illicit worship (e.g., 2 Kings 23.5), to the faithful they testified to God's power (e.g., Ps 8.3).
who makes destruction flash out against the strong, so that destruction comes upon the fortress.
5.8–9: The second doxology (see 4.13n.).
They hate the one who reproves in the gate, and they abhor the one who speaks the truth.

Therefore because you trample on the poor and take from them levies of grain, you have built houses of hewn stone, but you shall not live in them; you have planted pleasant vineyards, but you shall not drink their wine.
11 : A futility curse, in which the connections between actions and their expected effects are broken; cf. Deut 28.30–31,38–46; Mic 6.14–15; Zeph 1.13.
For I know how many are your transgressions, and how great are your sins— you who afflict the righteous, who take a bribe, and push aside the needy in the gate.
Therefore the prudent will keep silent in such a time; for it is an evil time.
10–13 : At the very site of legal proceedings, the city , the truth is rejected, the poor are plundered, and witnesses are bribed.
Seek good and not evil, that you may live; and so the Lord, the God of hosts, will be with you, just as you have said.
Hate evil and love good, and establish justice in the gate; it may be that the Lord, the God of hosts, will be gracious to the remnant of Joseph.
14–15 : Another plea for repentance; see vv. 4–7; , see 5.6n.

Therefore thus says the Lord, the God of hosts, the Lord: In all the squares there shall be wailing; and in all the streets they shall say, “Alas! alas!” They shall call the farmers to mourning, and those skilled in lamentation, to wailing;
16 : , professional mourners; see Jer 9.17–21; Ezek 32.16; Eccl 12.5; 2 Chr 35.25.
in all the vineyards there shall be wailing, for I will pass through the midst of you, says the Lord.
5.10–17: Amos condemns a corrupt legal system.
16–17 : Staying with the images of village life and returning to the motif of lament (5.1–3), Amos depicts the divine punishment for injustice.
Alas for you who desire the day of the Lord! Why do you want the day of the Lord? It is darkness, not light;
as if someone fled from a lion, and was met by a bear; or went into the house and rested a hand against the wall, and was bitten by a snake.
Is not the day of the Lord darkness, not light, and gloom with no brightness in it?

5.18–20: The day of the Lord in which Israelites expected to be vindicated against their enemies, will be, as depicted already in 1.1–2.16, a day when Israel too receives its judgment. See also Isa 13.6,9; Jer 46.10; Joel 2.1–2; Mal 4.5.
I hate, I despise your festivals, and I take no delight in your solemn assemblies.
Even though you offer me your burnt offerings and grain offerings, I will not accept them; and the offerings of well-being of your fatted animals I will not look upon.
Take away from me the noise of your songs; I will not listen to the melody of your harps.

21–23 : The language is harsh. refers to the three pilgrimage feasts (Ex 23.14–17; 34.18,22–23; Deut 16.16), as does (Lev 23.36; Deut 16.8). The sacrifices in v. 22 were prescribed; see, e.g., Lev 7.8–14. is often used of hymns (see 8.3), such as the psalms (e.g., see title of Ps 120). For a similar view, see Isa 1.11–17.
But let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.
24 : and , care for the poor. An is a riverbed that never fails, as opposed to a wadi, common in Israel's landscape, which had runoff water only in the rainy season.
Did you bring to me sacrifices and offerings the forty years in the wilderness, O house of Israel?

25 : As in Jeremiah (2.2–3) and Hosea (2.14–20; 9.10), the period in the wilderness after the Exodus is recalled as an ideal. Like Jer 7.21–23, the rhetorical question presumes that sacrifice was not a component of Israel's religious system immediately after the Exodus. This is inconsistent with other biblical traditions (contrast Ex 20–Num 10).
You shall take up Sakkuth your king, and Kaiwan your star-god, your images, which you made for yourselves;
26 : and (Saturn) designate known Assyrian deities. may be the same as the “Succoth” whose cult the Babylonians established in Samaria after its fall (2 Kings 17.30).
therefore I will take you into exile beyond Damascus, says the Lord, whose name is the God of hosts.
3.1–6.14: Judgment speeches against Israel come in three sections, each with the same opening (3.1; 4.1; 5.1).
5.18–6.14: A series of “woe” sayings (5.18–25; 6.1–3; 6.4–14) complete the section of judgment speeches.
5.21–27: The Lord delights not in abundance of festivals and sacrifices but in justice and righteousness. See also Isa 1.10–17; Mic 6.6–8; Ps 50.
